title: Plan a Stage 3 Mathematics Exhibition
overview: Bring together everything from Stage 3 Mathematics to design a mathematics exhibition that teaches visitors the most important ideas from this stage.

project.drivingQuestion: How can we use the mathematics from Stage 3 to plan a clear, accurate and welcoming exhibition for our school or community?
project.finalProduct: Create an exhibition plan with labelled displays, a schedule, a budget, a visitor survey with a data display, and a short explanation of your mathematical decisions.

stages.title: 1. Term 1 foundations display
stages.prompt: Choose the two most important ideas from Term 1 (Units 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6). Build a display that teaches each idea with a model, an example and a check question.
stages.evidence: Two labelled displays with models, examples and check questions

stages.title: 2. Term 2 applications corner
stages.prompt: Design an activity corner where visitors use Term 2 skills (Units 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12) to solve one real problem, showing every step of the method.
stages.evidence: A worked real problem with each method step shown

stages.title: 3. Term 3 connections wall
stages.prompt: Create a connections wall that links Term 3 ideas (Units 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18) to earlier units, with at least three labelled connections.
stages.evidence: A connections wall with three labelled links between units

stages.title: 4. Present and explain
stages.prompt: Present your exhibition plan. Explain at least three mathematical choices, check that your answers are sensible and identify one improvement you would make.
stages.evidence: Spoken, written or recorded mathematical explanation

evidenceChecklist: A worked real problem with method steps
A connections wall with three links
A schedule and budget for the exhibition
A survey with organised data display

rubric.secure: Calculations, measures, shapes, data and models are accurate and checked.
The plan connects ideas from several Stage 3 units for a clear purpose.
Labels, diagrams, tables or charts make the mathematics visible.
Decisions are explained using appropriate mathematical words and evidence.

questions.question: What is the value of the 6 in the number 462?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 1 methods.
questions.explanation: Name the place first. The 6 sits in the tens place. So its value is 6 tens = 60. (The 4 is worth 400 and the 2 is worth 2, and indeed 400 + 60 + 2 = 462.)

questions.question: Estimate 328 + 271 by rounding each number to the nearest 10.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 1 methods.
questions.explanation: 328 rounds to 330 (ones digit 8, round up). 271 rounds to 270 (ones digit 1, round down). So the estimate is 330 + 270 = 600. The real answer is 599, so our estimate is excellent – that is how we know it makes sense.

questions.question: Here is some data: 15, 9, 10, 8, 3. What is the largest value?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.unitTitle: Statistics: Tally Charts and Frequency Tables

questions.question: Here is some data: 20, 10, 18, 14, 7. What is the range?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.unitTitle: Statistics: Tally Charts and Frequency Tables

questions.question: Calculate 583 + 19.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
questions.explanation: Ones: 3 + 9 = 12, write 2, carry 1. Tens: 8 + 1 + 1 (carried) = 10, write 0, carry 1. Hundreds: 5 + 1 (carried) = 6. The answer is 602. Two carries in one sum – handled calmly, one column at a time.

questions.question: Calculate 725 + 275.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 3 methods.
questions.explanation: Ones: 5 + 5 = 10, write 0, carry 1. Tens: 2 + 7 + 1 = 10, write 0, carry 1. Hundreds: 7 + 2 + 1 = 10, write 0, carry 1 into a new thousands place. The answer is 1,000.

questions.question: Use the grid method to calculate 27 × 5.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
questions.explanation: Split 27 into 20 and 7. Then 20 × 5 = 100 and 7 × 5 = 35. Add the partial products: 100 + 35 = 135. So 27 × 5 = 135.

questions.question: Use the grid method to calculate 32 × 24.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 11 methods.
questions.explanation: Split 32 into 30 and 2, and 24 into 20 and 4. The four partial products are: 30 × 20 = 600, 2 × 20 = 40, 30 × 4 = 120, and 2 × 4 = 8. Add them: 600 + 40 + 120 + 8 = 768. So 32 × 24 = 768.

questions.question: Which fraction is equivalent to 1/2?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2, so they are equivalent.

questions.question: Which fraction is larger: 1/2 or 1/4?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: Halves are bigger than quarters, so 1/2 is larger than 1/4.

questions.question: Here is some data: 14, 8, 19, 11, 17, 14. Which value appears most often (the mode)?

questions.explanation: 14 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 14 appears more than any other value, so it is the mode.
